stories.headline: Citi banker issues stark warning: AI capitalism could face 'tragic end' without immediate action
stories.summary: Citi's Jay Collins warns that artificial intelligence and robotics pose an existential threat to capitalism that requires urgent response. The top banker emphasizes that the disruptive impact of rapidly advancing AI technology on economic systems is approaching faster than anticipated.
stories.original_title: A top Citi banker warns AI could mean a 'tragic end' for capitalism if we don't act now — AI and robotics' threat to capitalism is coming fast and requires an immediate response, Citi's Jay Collins told BI.
stories.context: Capitalism is the economic system where private companies and individuals own businesses and compete to make money by selling goods and services. Artificial intelligence and robotics represent machines getting so smart that they can do human tasks, from writing code to manufacturing products, potentially replacing millions of workers entirely. The core concern is that if machines can do almost everything cheaper than humans, the whole cycle of people earning wages from jobs and then spending that money back into the economy could break down completely. This banker is sounding the alarm that the world hasn't prepared for how quickly this might happen.
stories.why_it_matters: A top banker is warning that robots and smart computers could destroy our current money system because they might replace most workers faster than we expect. If businesses don't prepare for this chaos now, we could see massive job losses and an economic collapse that nobody knows how to fix.
stories.other_side: Critics say this is just scare talk because technology has always changed how we work without destroying capitalism, and businesses will naturally adapt as new opportunities appear. They believe fears are exaggerated and real solutions will emerge naturally without panicking.

stories.headline: Wolters Kluwer completes share buyback of 107,852 ordinary shares
stories.summary: Wolters Kluwer repurchased 107,852 of its own ordinary shares during the week of March 19-25, 2026, as part of its ongoing share buyback program. The transaction represents the company's continued return of capital to shareholders through direct market purchases.
stories.original_title: Share Buyback Transaction Details March 19 – March 25, 2026 — Wolters Kluwer today reports that it has repurchased 107,852 of own ordinary shares in the period from March 19, 2026, up to and including March 25, 2026...
stories.context: When a public company sells stock to investors, those shares represent tiny pieces of ownership in the business that anyone can buy and sell. A share buyback happens when a company decides to use its own money to purchase some of those shares back from the open market, essentially canceling them out. This makes each remaining share more valuable because there are fewer total shares available, and it's a way for companies to give money back to investors instead of paying regular dividends. Wolters Kluwer, which provides professional information and software services, is just following through on a planned program to gradually buy back pieces of itself.
stories.why_it_matters: A company is buying back its own stock, which typically rewards investors by making each remaining share more valuable and showing confidence in the business. This could matter to you if you own stock or invest in similar companies, as it affects how much money gets returned to regular people versus being reinvested in the company.
stories.other_side: Some argue that this money should instead be used to pay workers higher wages or invest in growing the business, rather than boosting stock prices for already-wealthy shareholders. They also worry that companies sometimes buy back stock just to make their numbers look better temporarily.

stories.headline: Washington becomes latest state to tax ultra-wealthy amid growing inequality movement
stories.summary: States are increasingly implementing taxes on the ultra-rich as federal action remains stalled, with Washington joining the trend. Patriotic Millionaires' Chuck Collins describes it as a growing movement to address wealth inequality through state-level tax policy.
stories.original_title: More States Are Taxing the Ultra-Rich—Washington Is the Latest — As inequality grows, states are stepping up where the federal government hasn't. 'It's a movement,' says Patriotic Millionaires' Chuck Collins.
stories.context: In modern economies, governments collect taxes to pay for public services, but the question of how heavily to tax the richest people has always been controversial. In the United States, the federal government in Washington, D.C., has been unable to pass new taxes on the ultra-wealthy due to political disagreements, leaving individual states to take action instead. The "ultra-rich" typically refers to households with net worth in the tens of millions or billions, often paying lower effective tax rates than regular workers due to various deductions and investment income rules. This movement argues that state-level taxes on extreme wealth can reduce inequality while funding public services, even if Congress won't act nationally.
stories.why_it_matters: States like Washington are now taxing the richest people because the federal government isn't doing anything about the gap between the ultra-wealthy and everyone else. This could spread across the country and might eventually force the federal government to take similar action against extreme wealth inequality.
stories.other_side: Opponents argue that these taxes will drive the rich people and businesses away to other states with lower taxes, hurting the local economy and jobs. They also say it's unfair to target successful people just because they have more money than others.

stories.headline: ECB's de Guindos outlines path forward amid European economic turbulence
stories.summary: European Central Bank Vice President Luis de Guindos addresses the challenges facing Europe's economy and the central bank's role in maintaining price stability. The speech focuses on navigating current economic uncertainties while preserving the euro's purchasing power.
stories.original_title: Luis de Guindos: Navigating turbulence: challenges for Europe and the path ahead — The European Central Bank (ECB) is the central bank of the European Union countries which have adopted the euro. Our main task is to maintain price stability in the euro area and so preserve the purchasing power of the single currency.
stories.context: The European Central Bank is essentially Europe's version of the Federal Reserve—a powerful institution that controls monetary policy for the 20 countries using the euro currency. Their main job is maintaining price stability, which means keeping inflation steady so money doesn't lose its purchasing power too quickly or become worthless. However, Europe faces tough challenges right now including slow growth, high energy prices, and economic uncertainty that make this careful balancing act extremely difficult. The Vice President is signaling that despite these economic storms, the central bank must stay focused on preventing prices from spiraling out of control.
stories.why_it_matters: Europe's central bank leader is trying to figure out how to keep prices from getting too crazy while the economy faces big problems, because their decisions affect whether everyday items become more expensive or cheaper for millions of people.
stories.other_side: Critics worry that if the bank acts too aggressively to control prices, it could slow down the economy too much and cost people their jobs. Others say the bank isn't doing enough and needs to take stronger action before problems get worse.

stories.headline: Wall Street bonuses hit record $246,900 in 2025, but outlook darkens for 2026
stories.summary: Wall Street bonuses reached an all-time high of $246,900 in 2025 as profits surged 30%, marking a peak year for financial sector compensation. However, tariffs and geopolitical instability threaten to end the bonus boom and dim prospects for 2026.
stories.original_title: Wall Street bonuses hit an all-time record in 2025 — but the outlook for 2026 is already darkening — The average Wall Street bonus climbed to $246,900 last year as profits surged 30%. Now tariffs and geopolitical turmoil are threatening to end the party.
stories.context: Wall Street is the nickname for America's financial industry in New York City, where banks and investment firms make money by helping companies trade stock, borrow money, and manage wealth. Bonuses are typically large year-end payments on top of regular salaries, and they directly reflect how much profit these companies made during the year—so when profits surge 30%, record payouts follow naturally. However, the financial industry is extremely sensitive to global tensions, especially when political conflicts or trade barriers (called tariffs) make business unpredictable and threaten to reduce future profits. This creates a cycle where boom years are followed by sudden downturns, and 2026 looks like it might be that downturn after 2025's peak.
stories.why_it_matters: Wall Street bankers made insane amounts of money last year, getting paid nearly a quarter-million dollars extra just in bonuses. This matters because it shows how unequal the economy has become, where a small group of workers make huge money while regular people struggle.
stories.other_side: Defenders say these workers earned their pay by generating huge profits for their companies and the overall economy, and cutting bonuses would drive away talented workers to other countries or industries. They also point out that bank jobs come with high pressure and long hours.

stories.headline: Ethereum price falls below $2,200 as bullish reversal signals emerge
stories.summary: Ethereum has slipped to under $2,200, but on-chain metrics indicate weakening selling pressure and potential investor accumulation. Analysts suggest a break above the $2,300 resistance level could trigger significant upward momentum for the cryptocurrency.
stories.original_title: Ethereum price drops below $2,200, but a bullish reversal is brewing — Ethereum (ETH) price shows early signs of a potential bullish trend reversal. On-chain data suggests accumulation and weakening selling pressure. A break above $2,300 could trigger further upside momentum. Ethereum has slipped below the $2,200 mark, but the b…
stories.context: Ethereum is the second-largest cryptocurrency after Bitcoin, a type of digital money that isn't controlled by any government or central bank. Like all cryptocurrencies, its price constantly fluctuates based on how many people want to buy versus sell it at any given moment, often swinging wildly between high and low values. When the price drops below important milestones like $2,200, it makes some investors nervous, but analysts look at technical patterns to guess which way prices will move next. The idea here is that if Ethereum can break past $2,300—a psychological price barrier where many investors might sell—it could signal confidence and trigger broader buying interest.
stories.why_it_matters: The price of Ethereum has dropped below a key level, but some signs hint that investors might start buying it up again soon, which could send the price soaring. This matters to anyone who owns crypto or is thinking about investing, as the price could swing dramatically in either direction.
stories.other_side: Skeptics argue that cryptocurrency is basically gambling with no real value backing it up, and the price could crash at any moment for no obvious reason. They also point out that regulators might soon crack down on crypto, which could wipe out a lot of investor money.

stories.headline: Indian NBFCs grow cautious as West Asia conflict fuels funding and credit concerns
stories.summary: Non-banking financial companies in India are adopting a cautious stance due to the ongoing war in West Asia, which could trigger broader inflation, demand, and credit cycle effects. While immediate impact remains limited, prolonged conflict poses significant risks to funding costs and credit availability.
stories.original_title: NBFCs turn cautious as West Asia war raises funding and credit concerns — While the immediate impact of the ongoing war in West Asia remains limited, non-bank lenders believe the real risk lies in a prolonged conflict, which could trigger second-order effects across inflation, demand and credit cycles.
stories.context: NBFC stands for Non-Banking Financial Companies, which are financial institutions in India that provide loans and credit just like regular banks do, but they don't have a banking license and can't accept traditional deposits from regular people. These companies rely on borrowing money from other financial institutions to lend out to customers, making them sensitive to changes in how expensive it is for them to get that funding. When conflicts break out in West Asia (the Middle East), it often drives up global oil prices, which can lead to higher inflation and make borrowing money more expensive throughout the world. The ripple effect is that NBFCs become worried about both their ability to get affordable funding and whether their borrowers can repay loans if the overall economy slows down.
stories.why_it_matters: Indian money lenders are getting nervous because the war in the Middle East could make everything more expensive and cause a domino effect on the whole economy. This could make it harder for regular people to get loans and push up the cost of borrowing money across India.
stories.other_side: Others say this worry is overblown because the Indian economy is big and diverse enough to handle problems from a conflict halfway across the world. They also point out that businesses are used to dealing with global problems and know how to adjust to changing situations.

stories.headline: AstaGuru Auction House expands into UK market with London base
stories.summary: Indian auction house AstaGuru has established its first international outpost in London, aiming to capitalize on Europe's growing collector market. The company has appointed industry veterans Laryssa Jesse and Tim Bourne to lead the UK expansion, with plans for an inaugural auction on May 27, 2026.
stories.original_title: London calling: AstaGuru Auction House enters UK market with first auction in May — Indian auction house AstaGuru has launched its first UK base in London, aiming to tap into Europe's growing collector market. Appointing Laryssa Jesse and Tim Bourne to lead the expansion, the company plans its inaugural UK auction on May 27, 2026, featuring …
stories.context: An auction house is a company that sells valuable items—like fine art, jewelry, or collectibles—to the highest bidder in public sales events. AstaGuru is an Indian company that established itself by connecting Indian sellers and buyers of luxury goods and art through competitive auctions. Expanding internationally means setting up operations in a new country (in this case, London) to reach wealthier collectors who might be interested in buying rare items from around the world. Hiring experienced industry veterans to run this new operation shows AstaGuru is serious about competing with established auction houses that have dominated European markets for decades.
stories.why_it_matters: An Indian auction company is expanding to London to sell art and collectibles to wealthy buyers around the world, showing how Indian businesses are growing globally. This could bring more money into India and help Indian art and culture get more attention and higher prices on the international stage.
stories.other_side: Competitors might struggle to compete with this new player, which could squeeze their profits and potentially reduce variety in the auction market. There's also risk that the company might fail in Europe, wasting money that could have been used to grow their business at home.

stories.headline: Persian Gulf war threatens economic stability and Strait of Hormuz shipping
stories.summary: The ongoing conflict in the Middle East, particularly the potential closure of the Strait of Hormuz, poses significant risks to the global economy. Economic uncertainty is compounded by unpredictable foreign policy that could disrupt critical oil shipping lanes and international trade.
stories.original_title: What Next - The Iran War Hits Your Pocket — Trump's unpredictability injects uncertainty into the economy, foreign policy, and everything else he touches. Even as his war messaging varies wildly moment to moment, the world economy is certain of one thing: it's bad for the Strait of Hormuz to close.Gues…
stories.context: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman that serves as a critical ocean passage for oil tankers carrying petroleum from the Middle East to the rest of the world. Roughly 20% of all global oil supply passes through this choke point, making it one of the most strategically important shipping lanes on Earth. If conflict in the region causes countries to block ships from using this passage, oil supplies would suddenly drop dramatically, causing energy prices to skyrocket throughout many countries. This explains why violence in this specific region creates panic in global markets—everyone relies on that oil flowing freely to keep economies running smoothly.
stories.why_it_matters: The war in the Middle East could shut down a major shipping route that carries around one-fifth of the world's oil, causing gas prices to skyrocket and hurting everyone's wallet. This would affect everything from how much you pay at the pump to the cost of shipping goods worldwide.
stories.other_side: Some argue that oil producers would quickly find other ways to get their oil to market, and the global economy could adapt faster than people expect to a temporary shipping problem. They also point out that we have oil reserves and other energy sources that could help us through a short-term disruption.

stories.headline: Housing think tank warns Coalition that construction targets fail to close demand gap
stories.summary: An economic think tank has issued a reality check to the Coalition government, projecting that housing completions for this year and next will remain below necessary levels. The analysis indicates current targets are insufficient to address the persistent supply-demand imbalance in the housing market.
stories.original_title: Housing reality check for Coalition — Think tank says projected completions for this year and next 'remain below level needed' to start closing demand-supply gap
stories.context: Housing markets work on the basic principle of supply and demand: when more people need homes than are available for sale, prices rise sharply, making it harder for regular people to afford buying a house. Governments often set construction targets as goals for how many new homes they want built each year, aiming to keep up with growing populations and increasing demand for housing. However, actually building houses takes years of planning, approval, construction, and materials—and many projects face delays, rising costs, or labor shortages that prevent targets from being met. This think tank is pointing out that even if construction goals are achieved, they still won't be enough to solve the fundamental shortage of homes compared to the number of people who need them.
stories.why_it_matters: The government isn't building enough homes to keep up with the number of people who need them, which means home prices and rents will keep getting more expensive and harder to afford. This matters because younger generations are getting priced out of homeownership and forced to rent for decades or live with their parents.
stories.other_side: Some argue that the government shouldn't interfere in the housing market because it might push prices up even more, and free markets should naturally balance supply and demand on their own. They also say that building too fast could lead to poor construction or create ghost towns if demand suddenly drops.
